Imaging genomics of cancer: a bibliometric analysis and review
==============================================================

*   [Xinyi Gou](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#auth-Xinyi-Gou-Aff1)[ORCID: orcid.org/0009-0001-9066-2635](https://orcid.org/0009-0001-9066-2635)[1](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#Aff1), 
*   [Aobo Feng](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#auth-Aobo-Feng-Aff2)[2](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#Aff2), 
*   [Caizhen Feng](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#auth-Caizhen-Feng-Aff1)[1](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#Aff1), 
*   [Jin Cheng](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#auth-Jin-Cheng-Aff1)[ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-1199-1351](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1199-1351)[1](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#Aff1)&
*   …
*   [Nan Hong](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#auth-Nan-Hong-Aff1)[1](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#Aff1)

Show authors
[_Cancer Imaging_](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/)**volume 25**, Article number:24 (2025) [Cite this article](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#citeas)

*   1606 Accesses

*   [Metrics details](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9/metrics)

Abstract
--------

### Background

Imaging genomics is a burgeoning field that seeks to connections between medical imaging and genomic features. It has been widely applied to explore heterogeneity and predict responsiveness and disease progression in cancer. This review aims to assess current applications and advancements of imaging genomics in cancer.

### Methods

Literature on imaging genomics in cancer was retrieved and selected from PubMed, Web of Science, and Embase before July 2024. Detail information of articles, such as systems and imaging features, were extracted and analyzed. Citation information was extracted from Web of Science and Scopus. Additionally, a bibliometric analysis of the included studies was conducted using the Bibliometrix R package and VOSviewer.

### Results

A total of 370 articles were included in the study. The annual growth rate of articles on imaging genomics in cancer is 24.88%. China (133) and the USA (107) were the most productive countries. The top 2 keywords plus were “survival” and “classification”. The current research mainly focuses on the central nervous system (121) and the genitourinary system (110, including 44 breast cancer articles). Despite different systems utilizing different imaging modalities, more than half of the studies in each system employed radiomics features.

### Conclusions

Publication databases provide data support for imaging genomics research. The development of artificial intelligence algorithms, especially in feature extraction and model construction, has significantly advanced this field. It is conducive to enhancing the related-models’ interpretability. Nonetheless, challenges such as the sample size and the standardization of feature extraction and model construction must overcome. And the research trends revealed in this study will guide the development of imaging genomics in the future and contribute to more accurate cancer diagnosis and treatment in the clinic.

Advances in knowledge
---------------------

Imaging genomics is considered a crucial component in cancer precision medicine. A review is necessary to offer a holistic and systematic view of this field.

Focused on survival and classification, imaging genomics shows potential in cancer heterogeneity, yet faces challenges on standardization, model complexity, and ethics.

This study clarifies the objectives, current standing, and evolution of imaging genomics in cancer, offering a basic workflow and trends for cancer analysis in this domain.

Background
----------

Cancer remains a devastating global health challenge, exerting a significant and escalating toll on societies and healthcare systems worldwide. The Global Cancer Statistics 2022 report reveals that in 2022, nearly 20 million individuals were diagnosed with cancer, resulting in 9.7 million cancer-related deaths [[1](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR1 "Bray F, Laversanne M, Sung H, Ferlay J, Siegel RL, Soerjomataram I, et al. Global cancer statistics 2022: GLOBOCAN estimates of incidence and mortality worldwide for 36 cancers in 185 countries. CA Cancer J Clin. 2024;74(3):229–63.")].

Diagnostic imaging techniques such as Computed Tomography (CT), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), Positron Emission Tomography (PET), and Ultrasound (US) are integral to the detection, staging, and monitoring of cancer treatment responses [[2](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR2 "Panayides AS, Pattichis MS, Leandrou S, Pitris C, Constantinidou A, Pattichis CS. Radiogenomics for Precision Medicine with a Big Data Analytics Perspective. IEEE J Biomedical Health Informat. 2019;23(5):2063–79."),[3](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR3 "Korn R. Novel multi-modal endpoints uncovered by radiogenomics integrating CT or MRI data. Ann Oncol. 2015;26(Korn R Imaging Endpoints Core Lab, Scottsdale, AZ, United States):ii13."),[4](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR4 "Garcia-Figueiras R, Baleato-Gonzalez S, Luna A, Munoz-Iglesias J, Oleaga L, Vallejo Casas JA, et al. Assessing immunotherapy with functional and molecular imaging and Radiomics. Radiographics. 2020;40(7):1987–2010.")]. They can perform non-invasive multiple scans, providing information about the cancer and surrounding tissues, and can be compared for changes over time. With the advancement of artificial intelligence algorithms, the scope of imaging features has expanded beyond conventional imaging features, such as tumor size, shape, and margin, to encompass high-throughput, algorithm-based radiomics features [[5](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR5 "Ferro M, de Cobelli O, Musi G, del Giudice F, Carrieri G, Busetto GM, et al. Radiomics in prostate cancer: an up-to-date review. Ther Adv Urol. 2022;14:17562872221109020."), [6](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR6 "Mayerhoefer ME, Materka A, Langs G, Häggström I, Szczypiński P, Gibbs P, et al. Introduction to radiomics. J Nucl Med. 2020;61(4):488–95.")]. These mainly include handcrafted radiomic, and deep learning features, which may not be perceptible to the human eye [[7](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR7 "Chartrand G, Cheng PM, Vorontsov E, Drozdzal M, Turcotte S, Pal CJ, et al. Deep learning: a primer for radiologists. Radiographics. 2017;37(7):2113–31.")].

In addition to imaging technology, advances in genomics have also provided new perspectives for cancer research, and the combination of the two has given birth to the interdisciplinary discipline of imaging genomics. Imaging genomics, also known as radiogenomics, is an emerging field that lies at the intersection of medical imaging and genomics [[8](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR8 "Singh S, Mohajer B, Wells SA, Garg T, Hanneman K, Takahashi T, et al. Imaging Genomics and Multiomics: a guide for beginners starting Radiomics-Based Research. Acad Radiol. 2024;31(6):2281–91."), [9](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR9 "Hartmann K, Sadée CY, Satwah I, Carrillo-Perez F, Gevaert O. Imaging genomics: data fusion in uncovering disease heritability. Trends Mol Med. 2023;29(2):141–51.")]. The study of genomics involves analyzing the complete genetic makeup, encompassing DNA examination, transcriptomics (which includes the investigation of mRNAs, miRNAs, and lncRNAs), and epigenomics research [[10](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR10 "Menghi F, Liu ET. Functional genomics of complex cancer genomes. Nat Commun. 2022;13(1):5908."), [11](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR11 "Onoyama T, Ishikawa S, Isomoto H. Gastric cancer and genomics: review of literature. J Gastroenterol. 2022;57(8):505–16.")]. The term radiogenomics, which first emerged in 2002, has expanded beyond its initial definition [[12](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR12 "Yard BD, Adams DJ, Chie EK, Tamayo P, Battaglia JS, Gopal P, et al. A genetic basis for the variation in the vulnerability of cancer to DNA damage. Nat Commun. 2016;7:11428."), [13](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR13 "Pinker K, Shitano F, Sala E, Do RK, Young RJ, Wibmer AG, et al. Background, current role and potential applications of radiogenomics. J Magn Reson Imaging. 2018;47(3):604–20.")]. Originally, it described research examining the relationship between patient genetics and variations in their sensitivity to radiation therapy. However, its content has now been expanded to include imaging genomics. The primary objective of imaging genomics is to identify and establish relationships between both image features (semantic and quantitative) with genomic information, thereby constructing association maps that can further be correlated with clinical outcomes or other relevant metrics [[13](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR13 "Pinker K, Shitano F, Sala E, Do RK, Young RJ, Wibmer AG, et al. Background, current role and potential applications of radiogenomics. J Magn Reson Imaging. 2018;47(3):604–20."), [14](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR14 "Liu Z, Wu K, Wu B, Tang X, Yuan H, Pang H, Imaging genomics for accurate diagnosis and treatment of tumors: A cutting edge overview. Biomed Pharmacother., Wu Z, Wu K, Tang B, Yuan X, Pang H, Huang H, Zhu Y et al. X., xzhu@gdmu.edu.cn; Luo H., luohui@gdmu.edu.cn; Qi Y., qiyi7272@gdmu.edu.cn) Southern Marine Science and Engineering Guangdong Laboratory (Zhanjiang), Zhanjiang, China):111173.")]. By creating a comprehensive map of associations, it can enhance the precision of prognostic and predictive models across a range of oncological conditions. This integration of imaging data with genomic insights holds the potential to improve patient stratification and treatment planning [[15](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR15 "Bodalal Z, Trebeschi S, Nguyen-Kim TDL, Schats W, Beets-Tan R. Radiogenomics: bridging imaging and genomics. Abdom Radiol (NY). 2019;44(6):1960–84."),[16](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR16 "Wang Y, Wang Y, Guo C, Xie X, Liang S, Zhang R, et al. Cancer genotypes prediction and associations analysis from imaging phenotypes: a survey on radiogenomics. Biomark Med. 2020;14(12):1151–64."),[17](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR17 "Mendes Serrão E, Klug M, Moloney BM, Jhaveri A, Lo Gullo R, Pinker K, et al. Current status of Cancer Genomics and Imaging phenotypes: what radiologists need to know. Radiol Imaging Cancer. 2023;5(6):e220153.")].

The underlying mechanism of imaging features can be discovered by imaging genomics, which may enhance the credibility of its clinical value and provide potential target of therapeutic intervention [[18](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR18 "Gao B, Feng C, Chai F, Wei S, Hong N, Ye Y, et al. CT-detected extramural venous invasion-related gene signature for the overall survival prediction in patients with gastric cancer. Cancer Med. 2021;10(21):7816–30."),[19](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR19 "Ogbonnaya CN, Alsaedi BSO, Alhussaini AJ, Hislop R, Pratt N, Nabi G. Radiogenomics Reveals Correlation between Quantitative Texture Radiomic Features of Biparametric MRI and Hypoxia-Related Gene Expression in Men with Localised Prostate Cancer. J Clin Med [Internet]. 2023;12(7). Available from: 
                  https://www.embase.com/search/results?subaction=viewrecord%26id=L2022558771%26from=export
                  
                "),[20](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR20 "Colen RR, Vangel M, Wang J, Gutman DA, Hwang SN, Wintermark M et al. Imaging genomic mapping of an invasive MRI phenotype predicts patient outcome and metabolic dysfunction: A TCGA glioma phenotype research group project. BMC Med Genomics [Internet]. 2014;7(1). Available from: 
                  https://www.embase.com/search/results?subaction=viewrecord%26id=L53176027%26from=export
                  
                ")]. The formal classification of molecular subtypes through genomic analysis is both costly and time-consuming. In clinical practice, immunohistochemistry(IHC) methods are often employed as an alternative for molecular typing. Nevertheless, IHC specimens are typically extracted from a single localized region. Factors such as tissue preparation, tumor heterogeneity, or genetic variations affecting protein expression can result in discrepancies between IHC and genetic testing outcomes [[21](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR21 "Pan S, Cox H, Willmott J, Mundt E, Gorringe H, Landon M, et al. Discordance between germline genetic findings and abnormal tumor immunohistochemistry staining of mismatch repair proteins in individuals with suspected lynch syndrome. Front Oncol. 2023;13:1069467."), [22](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR22 "Smithgall MC, Remotti H, Hsiao SJ, Mansukhani M, Liu-Jarin X, Fernandes H. Investigation of discrepant mismatch repair immunohistochemistry and microsatellite instability polymerase chain reaction test results for gynecologic cancers using next-generation sequencing. Hum Pathol. 2022;119:41–50.")]. Discordance rates between genetic testing and IHC classifications can indeed vary significantly, with reported rates ranging from about 40–100% across different studies [[23](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR23 "Dix-Peek T, Phakathi BP, van den Berg EJ, Dickens C, Augustine TN, Cubasch H, et al. Discordance between PAM50 intrinsic subtyping and immunohistochemistry in South African women with breast cancer. Breast Cancer Res Treat. 2023;199(1):1–12."),[24](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR24 "Cho N. Breast Cancer Radiogenomics: Association of Enhancement Pattern at DCE MRI with Deregulation of mTOR Pathway. Radiology. 2020;296(2):288–9."),[25](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR25 "Kim HK, Park KH, Kim Y, Park SE, Lee HS, Lim SW, et al. Discordance of the PAM50 intrinsic subtypes compared with immunohistochemistry-based surrogate in breast Cancer patients: potential implication of genomic alterations of discordance. Cancer Res Treat. 2019;51(2):737–47.")]. Through imaging genomics, it becomes possible to predict molecular typing and therapeutic efficacy noninvasively.

Therefore, imaging genomics research has provided valuable insights into cancer [[26](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR26 "Hoshino I, Yokota H. Radiogenomics of gastroenterological cancer: the dawn of personalized medicine with artificial intelligence-based image analysis. Ann Gastroenterol Surg. 2021;5(4):427–35.")]. This review synthesizes the current state of imaging genomics across various cancer types, focusing on their basic workflow and challenges. Additionally, a bibliometric analysis quantitatively assessed the trajectory and focal points of imaging genomics in cancer.

Materials and methods
---------------------

### Search strategy

Data collection was conducted utilizing the PubMed, Embase, and Web of Science databases, and was enhanced by examining the reference lists of pertinent articles included in our review up to July 2024. The search query of PubMed and Web of Science is (“imaging genomics” OR ‘radiogenomics’) AND “cancer”. For Embase, the search string is (‘imaging genomics’/exp OR ‘imaging genomics’ OR ‘radiogenomics’/exp OR ‘radiogenomics’) AND (‘cancer’/exp OR ‘cancer’). It is noteworthy that the initial objective of radiogenomics was to identify potential genomic predictors for radiation toxicity; however, this aspect was not included in the scope of the study.

### Study selection

All articles were screened back-to-back by two independent investigators (XYG and ABF) to determine whether they met our inclusion criteria. Any disputes were solved by a discussion with a third reviewer (CZF). The inclusion criteria were as followed: (1) studies focusing on cancer patients; (2) Research utilizing imaging genomics methodologies; (3) Peer-reviewed and formally published articles. And the exclusion criteria were: (1) non-human studies; (2) Articles not written in English; (3) Review.

### Data extraction

Two investigators (XYG and ABF) extracted basic study information including author, year of publication, study design, sample size, and type of cancer. Subsequently, a more detailed extraction was performed to gather insights into the imaging genomics field, such as cancer types, imaging modalities, imaging features and genetic types. Imaging features included conventional imaging features and radiomics features.

Specifically, conventional imaging features refer to characteristics that can be directly observed, measured, and simply calculated from medical imaging, such as tumor size, shape, location, and signs (for example, mismatch on T2-FLAIR), as well as values obtained from special examinations like the apparent diffusion coefficient measurements from DWI sequences and the standardized uptake value max (SUVmax) values from PET scans. Radiomics features, on the other hand, are quantitative imaging characteristics derived from computer algorithms, such as handcrafted radiomics features based on the PyRadiomics platform and features extracted using a specific deep learning algorithm [[27](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR27 "van Timmeren JE, Cester D, Tanadini-Lang S, Alkadhi H, Baessler B. Radiomics in medical imaging—how-to guide and critical reflection. Insights into Imaging. 2020;11(1):91."),[28](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR28 "Zhang W, Guo Y, Jin Q. Radiomics and its feature selection: a review. Symmetry. 2023;15(10):1834."),[29](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR29 "Bera K, Braman N, Gupta A, Velcheti V, Madabhushi A. Predicting cancer outcomes with radiomics and artificial intelligence in radiology. Nat REVIEWS Clin Oncol. 2022;19(2):132–46.")].

### Bibliometric analysis

Citation data was extracted from the Web of Science and Scopus database to perform a bibliometric analysis. It involved the keywords, keywords plus, quantification of publication trends, citation patterns, and collaborative efforts within the imaging genomics of cancer research. Keywords plus is a set of terms extracted from the titles of cited documents in an article. It aims to complement the author-provided keywords, enhancing the thoroughness and precision of literature searches. Additionally, local citations refer to the frequency with which a study is referenced by other researchers within the included articles. Global Citations, on the other hand, indicate the total number of times an article has been cited across all documents indexed in extensive bibliographic databases, such as Web of Science or Scopus. The data will be used to identify key publications and keywords in the research that require further investigation. Bibliometrix R package([https://github.com/massimoaria/bibliometrix](https://github.com/massimoaria/bibliometrix)) and VOSviewer were used to perform bibliometric analyses and visualization.

Results
-------

Published literature was retrieved from PubMed (_n_ = 572), Embase (_n_ = 1123) and Web of Science (_n_ = 964). Based on the inclusion and exclusion criteria, a total of 370 articles were selected for this review (Fig.[1](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#Fig1)).

**Fig. 1**

[![Image 4: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs40644-025-00841-9/MediaObjects/40644_2025_841_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9/figures/1)

Schematic workflow of this review

[Full size image](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9/figures/1)

### Annual publication output

From 2008 to June 2024, a total of 370 articles were published from 151 journals on the topic of cancer and imaging genomics. The data reveals a significant compound annual growth rate of 24.88%, indicating a rapidly increasing interest and research activity in this field. The internal co-authorship rate was 26.49%, with an average of 10.2 co-authors per document. A total of 10,528 references were cited across these publications.

### Affiliation and country

As shown in Fig.[2](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#Fig2)A, the most prolific source of published articles is the University of California, with a total of 74 publications. In second place, the University of Texas boasts 60 articles. Sharing the third position are the Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center and Stanford University, both contributing 50 articles each. Fudan University secures the fifth spot with 45 articles, closely followed by the Helmholtz Association with 44 articles. From the data presented, it is clear that the University of California leads in research output. This likely reflects the institution’s strong research capabilities and extensive academic resources in the relevant fields.

After an analysis of corresponding author’s countries, it was exhibited that a total of 27 countries/regions in included articles. As shown in Fig.[2](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#Fig2)B, the country that contributed the largest volume of publications (133) was the China, which accounted for 35.9% of the total. USA was ranked the second (107, 28.9%), Korea third (22, 5.9%), and followed by Italy(19, 5.1%) and Germany (17, 4.6%). Multiple country publications (MCP) revealed the publication number of co-authors from different countries/regions. Although the USA had the highest MCP (35), its MCP ratio (= MCP/articles) was 32.7%. The top 3 cited countries were USA(5194), Netherlands(3336) and China(2339). Countries with close cooperation include USA and CHINA (22), USA and Germany (10), USA and United Kingdom (9). This indicates that the United States plays a significant role in international scientific research cooperation and maintains particularly frequent collaborative relationships with China. The collaboration between the two countries likely facilitates the exchange of research resources and technology, thereby enhancing their respective research capabilities.

**Fig. 2**

[![Image 5: figure 2](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs40644-025-00841-9/MediaObjects/40644_2025_841_Fig2_HTML.png)](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9/figures/2)

**A**, Bar chart showing the number of articles published by the top 10 Affiliations; **B**, Bar chart displaying the number of articles published by the top 10 Countries, with SCP representing Single Country Publications and MCP representing Multiple Country Publications

[Full size image](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9/figures/2)

### Source journals

In the bibliometric analysis of primary source journals, “Cancers” has emerged as the most prolific journal, with a total of 28 articles, underscoring its pivotal role in oncology research publication. It is closely followed by “Scientific Reports”, which has contributed a notable 20 articles, reflecting its multidisciplinary research focus. Ranking third is “European Radiology”, which has contributed 19 articles, highlighting its substantial impact in the field of medical imaging. “Frontiers in Oncology” and “Radiology” are tied for the fourth position, each journal contributing 16 articles, respectively.

In terms of the most locally cited sources, “Radiology” leads with an impressive 885 citations. “European Radiology” and “Neuro-Oncology” are the second most cited with 330 citations, followed by “Clinical Cancer Research” with 329 citations, and “Scientific Reports” with 314 citations. The most globally cited document is a paper authored by Hugo J. W. L. Aerts and colleagues, which was published in the journal _Nature Communications_ in June 2014 and has garnered a significant global citation count of 3,281(Table[1](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#Tab1)) [[30](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR30 "Aerts HJWL, Velazquez ER, Leijenaar RTH, Parmar C, Grossmann P, Carvalho S, Decoding tumour phenotype by noninvasive imaging using a quantitative radiomics approach. Department of Radiation Oncology (MAASTRO), Research Institute GROW, Maastricht University, 6229ET Maastricht, Netherlands):4006.")]. This research represents an early application of imaging genomics, examining CT scans from 1019 patients with lung and head and neck cancers across seven distinct cohorts [[31](https://cancerimagingjournal.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s40644-025-00841-9#ref-CR31 "Aerts HJWL, Velazquez ER, Leijenaar RTH, Parmar C, Grossmann P, Carvalho S, et al. REVIEW ARTICLE

Cancer Genomics
===============================

Author links open overlay panel Marco A.Fonseca-Montaño a c, Susana Blancas a b c, Luis A.Herrera-Montalvo a, Alfredo Hidalgo-Miranda a c

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.arcmed.2022.11.011](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.arcmed.2022.11.011 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0188440922001539&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

In the past decade, genomics has fundamentally changed our view of [cancer biology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cancer-pathobiology "Learn more about cancer biology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), allowing comprehensive analyses of mutations, copy number alterations, structural variants, gene expression and [DNA methylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-methylation "Learn more about DNA methylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") profiles in large-scale studies across different [cancer types](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cancer-types "Learn more about cancer types from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Efforts like [The Cancer Genome Atlas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/the-cancer-genome-atlas "Learn more about The Cancer Genome Atlas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (TCGA) and the International Cancer Genome Consortium (ICGC) have fostered international collaborations for cancer genomic analyses and have generated public databases that give scientists around the world access to thoroughly curated data, which have been extensively used as a tool for further hypothesis driven research on several aspects of cancer biology. In parallel, some of these findings are being translated into specific clinical benefits for cancer patients. In this review, we provide a brief historical description of the evolution of international public [cancer genome projects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cancer-genome-project "Learn more about cancer genome projects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and related databases, as well as we discuss about their impact on general cancer research.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001679)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001424)

A Historical Perspective of Cancer Genomics
-------------------------------------------

Cancer is a disease of the genome. This became evident since the 1960’s through the discovery of specific genomic alterations related to particular [cancer types](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cancer-types "Learn more about cancer types from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by means of [cytogenetic analyses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/chromosome-analysis "Learn more about cytogenetic analyses from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0001)) and further confirmed in the 70’s, with the discovery of [oncogenes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/oncogene "Learn more about oncogenes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0002)), and later on the description of mutations related to oncogenic activation ([3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0003)). In this context, driver mutations were described as somatic alterations responsible for [malignant transformation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/malignant-transformation "Learn more about malignant transformation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), by conferring evolutionary selective advantages to malignant [cell subpopulations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cell-subpopulation "Learn more about cell subpopulations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0004),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0005)). In contrast, passenger mutations are somatic alterations that do not have a significative effect during the tumorigenesis process ([4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0004),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0005)).

During the 80’s, the discovery of novel oncogenes continued, in parallel with discussions about the feasibility and usefulness of the human [genome sequencing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/genome-sequencing "Learn more about genome sequencing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In 1986, Renato Dulbecco's perspective paper “A turning point in cancer research”, made clear that understanding of cancer's daunting complexity, required to focus on the sequence of the whole cellular genome. This perspective became one of the first clear support to the Human Genome Project and one of the first examples on how knowledge derived from [human genome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/human-genome "Learn more about human genome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") sequence might enhance our knowledge of the molecular basis of cancer ([6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0006)).

Just after the completion of the first draft of the Human Genome in 2001 ([7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0007)), in 2005 a working group was created to generate a recommendation on a “Human Cancer Genome Project”, with the goal to “obtain a comprehensive description of the [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") basis of human cancer” through the systematic identification and characterization of all genomic alterations associated with significant frequency in several human cancers, through the analysis of the genome sequence of ∼12,500 samples from 50 cancer types ([8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0008)). Just as the Human Genome Project, the Human [Cancer Genome Project](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cancer-genome-project "Learn more about Cancer Genome Project from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") faced criticism from the scientific community, particularly regarding aspects related to its cost, potential scientific and clinical impact ([9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0009)). The calculated costs of the project were around $12 billion dollars in 2005; however, based on the Human Genome Sequencing Project experience, the working group considered the reduction in costs derived from the advancement of sequencing technologies, a situation that became a reality with the development of massive parallel sequencing technologies. By the end of 2005, the US National Institutes of Health formally launched [The Cancer Genome Atlas](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/the-cancer-genome-atlas "Learn more about The Cancer Genome Atlas from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (TCGA), as a three year pilot project with a budget of $100 million dollars. In a 12 year period, the TCGA characterized more than 20,000 samples across 33 different cancer types, generating over 2.5 petabytes of data which include genomic, [epigenomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/epigenomics "Learn more about epigenomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [transcriptomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/transcriptomics "Learn more about transcriptomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [proteomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/proteomics "Learn more about proteomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") data ([https://www.cancer.gov/about-nci/organization/ccg/research/structural-genomics/tcga](https://www.cancer.gov/about-nci/organization/ccg/research/structural-genomics/tcga)). This trove of information was made available for the public since the beginning of the project, a situation that allowed scientists the data mining in search for new insights into [cancer biology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cancer-pathobiology "Learn more about cancer biology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

It soon became evident that the mammoth task of characterizing a higher number of tumor samples from different cancer types will require an international and coordinated effort. This led to the development of the International Cancer Genome Consortium (ICGC), an initiative launched in 2008 with the aim of coordinating large-scale [cancer genome sequencing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cancer-genome-sequencing "Learn more about cancer genome sequencing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") studies in 50 different tumor types provided by scientific efforts around the world ([10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0010)). Data generated from the TCGA was included in the ICGC effort and in its latest data release (March 27, 2019), the ICGC data portal includes 86 cancer projects from 22 primary anatomical sites. The total number of tissue donors for these projects is 24,289 and molecular information is available for 22,330 individuals. A total number of 81,782,588 [somatic mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/somatic-mutation "Learn more about somatic mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has been identified in this dataset, which constitutes the largest and most ambitious [cancer genome sequencing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cancer-genome-sequencing "Learn more about cancer genome sequencing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") project to date ([https://dcc.icgc.org/](https://dcc.icgc.org/)) ([11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0011),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0012)). A large amount of the generated data by the TCGA and ICGC was focused on sequencing the genome [coding regions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/coding-region "Learn more about coding regions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the [exome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/exome "Learn more about exome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This is due to the fact that [exome sequencing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/exome-sequencing "Learn more about exome sequencing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is cheaper than [Whole Genome Sequencing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/whole-genome-sequencing "Learn more about Whole Genome Sequencing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (WGS) and also due to the fact that biological interpretation of the impact of non-synonymous mutations in coding regions of the genome might be more straightforward, compared to mutations in regulatory elements and non-coding regions of the genome. However, several studies soon demonstrated the key role of mutations in non-coding and regulatory elements of the genome, such as mutations in the promoter of _TERT_ in [melanoma](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/nodular-melanoma "Learn more about melanoma from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and mutations in the promoter of _[FOXA1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/foxa1 "Learn more about FOXA1 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ in breast cancer ([13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0013),[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0014)), indicating the need to expand analyses to the whole cancer genome. This situation led to the creation of the Pan-Cancer Analysis of Whole Genomes (PCAWG) initiative within the ICGC. This is an international effort to identify common patterns of mutation in more than 2,600 cancer whole genomes. In 2020, the flagship paper of the TCGA/ICGC PCAWG consortium was published, together with a set of additional papers focused on diverse aspects, such as: patterns of [somatic mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/somatic-mutation "Learn more about somatic mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in human cancer, mutational signatures, tumor [genomic evolution](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/genomic-evolution "Learn more about genomic evolution from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), mutations in genomic non-coding regions, cancer [mitochondrial genome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mitochondrial-genome "Learn more about mitochondrial genome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and others

([https://www.nature.com/collections/afdejfafdb](https://www.nature.com/collections/afdejfafdb)). According to these analyses, a cancer genome contains 4–5 driver mutations in coding and non-coding regions, but interestingly, there is still a 5% of tumors where no known driver mutations were identified. This suggests that either our catalogue of cancer-causing mutations is still not complete, or that other types of oncogenic processes might play a key role in cancer development in these cancer types ([4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0004)).

The Role of Genomics in Cancer Research
---------------------------------------

The [cancer genome sequencing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cancer-genome-sequencing "Learn more about cancer genome sequencing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") also identified the common presence of catastrophic single events, affecting clustered regions of the genome, during early tumor development. The process of [chromothripsis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/chromothripsis "Learn more about chromothripsis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") generates many clustered structural aberrations, usually resulting in DNA copy number alterations (CNA), such as amplification of cancer-associated genes ([15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0015)). In this regard, [genome sequencing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/genome-sequencing "Learn more about genome sequencing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") efforts have contributed to increase our basic understanding of cancer and include the systematic description and identification of diverse types of [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") alterations (i.e. substitutions, [indels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/indel "Learn more about indels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), gene fusions and CNAs) leading to human cancer ([16.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0016), [17.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0017), [18.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0018), [19.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0019), [20.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0020), [21.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0021)). These projects also explored how aberrations at epigenetic, transcriptional and protein level ([22.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0022), [23.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0023), [24.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0024), [25.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0025), [26.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0026)) generate different functional impacts and complement DNA alterations in human cancer. In the epigenetic and transcriptional arena, the analyses of [microRNAs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/microrna "Learn more about microRNAs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and long-non coding RNAs (lncRNAs) have expanded our understanding on the establishment of complex regulatory networks in healthy tissues and how alterations in these networks contribute to cancer development ([27.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0027), [28.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0028), [29.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0029), [30.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0030)). Identification of this multitude of aberrations in the cancer genome, led to a comprehensive analysis of functional biological pathways in [cancer cells](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cancer-cell "Learn more about cancer cells from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and to the discovery of mutational signatures, allowing a better understanding about how they might be acting in a coordinated way during cancer development ([31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0031),[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0032)).

Basic insights into the cellular origin of cancer and alterations of common biological pathways might have a profound impact on how we classify and understand the early stages of tumor development. In this regard, the discovery of tumors from different anatomical sites that share common genomic alterations, which are biologically more similar than tumors from the same tissue, but not sharing common genetic aberrations, have led to the idea of a better classification of cancer. The presence of specific genomic alterations might represent a better way to classify tumors into biological and clinical entities, for example, tumors positive for HER2 amplification, or tumors that share common mutational signatures, compared to the current classification based on tissue of origin ([33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0033)).

Intra-tumoral Heterogeneity
---------------------------

Another biologically and clinically relevant property of tumors is intratumoral heterogeneity, which is manifested at genomic and cellular levels. Sequencing analyses of human cancers have shown that tumors are composed by a heterogeneous clonal landscape, and this intra-tumor heterogeneity has a profound impact on cancer progression, disease relapse, treatment response and resistance. Recent studies analyzed intra-tumoral heterogeneity in whole genome sequences of more than 2,640 samples from 38 [cancer types](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cancer-types "Learn more about cancer types from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and identified that 91.5% of these samples showed evidence of distinct sub-clonal expansion with positive selection of driver mutations associated with tumor evolution ([34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0034),[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0035)). The presence of clonal heterogeneity and even cancer associated driver [somatic mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/somatic-mutation "Learn more about somatic mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in subclones has even been detected in normal tissues, particularly in those with a high self-renewal rate. These tissues include the skin, blood, esophagus, [endometrium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/endometrium "Learn more about endometrium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [bladder](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/bladder "Learn more about bladder from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), colon and liver, indicating that normal tissues represent “mosaics” of sub-clonal cell populations, and that the presence of cancer-related mutations is not synonymous of cancer development. These studies also suggest that either the presence of other alterations is needed or the combination of specific cancer-causing mutations is required for tumor development ([36.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0036), [37.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0037), [38.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0038), [39.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0039), [40.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0040), [41.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0041), [42.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0042)). This is supported by other analytical approaches used in the PCAWG data, such as the analysis of evolutionary history of tumors. In this analysis, the genomic aberrations identified in a [tumor biopsy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/tumor-biopsy "Learn more about tumor biopsy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are taken as a snapshot of the tumor's evolutionary history, assuming that mutations which arose early in [tumor initiation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/tumor-initiation "Learn more about tumor initiation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") will be represented in a higher clonal fraction than mutations which were acquired later in tumor development. In this scenario, a “molecular time” can be defined to calculate the order and timing in which the mutations appeared in the cancer genome. Using this approach, the [whole genome sequencing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/whole-genome-sequencing "Learn more about whole genome sequencing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") analysis of 2,658 tumors from 38 cancer types were used to reconstruct the life history and evolution of mutational processes in human cancer. In accordance with the intra-tumoral and normal tissue clonal analysis, the evolutionary analysis identified that early driver mutations arise very early, often preceding cancer diagnosis by years or even decades. The study also identified a restricted group of commonly [mutated genes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mutated-genes "Learn more about mutated genes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in early cancer development, including highly recurrent mutated genes like _[KRAS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/kras "Learn more about KRAS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ _, TERT_ and _CDKN2A_, between others, indicating that these genes play important roles in early cell transformation and even in the development of precancerous lesions ([43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0043)).

Mutational Signatures
---------------------

Several efforts have been developed to identify the underlying patterns of mutations found in the cancer genome and to understand if different mutational processes and aetiological agents generate specific mutational patterns when cells are exposed to them during lifetime. This kind of analysis would allow the genome to be used as a record of environmental exposure to cancer-causing processes, which will leave specific “signatures” that might be explored as an archeological record to identify different mutational processes, which are active before and during tumor evolution. Historically, the correlation between specific mutation signatures and particular carcinogens was explored at the single gene level; however, for the first time, cancer sequencing data availability allowed the exploration of these correlations at the whole genome level in several cancer types ([44.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0044), [45.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0045), [46.](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0188440922001539#bib0046)). To explore this multidimensional data, mathematical approaches like non-negative matrix factorization have been able

enriched_points.expanded_content: A comprehensive assessment of existing research on cancer genomics, focusing on imaging genomics and genomic alterations, provides foundational knowledge and identifies gaps for further exploration.

Utilizing advanced statistical methods and bioinformatics tools, data analysis examines genomic data to uncover patterns, mutations, and associations critical for understanding cancer biology and treatment.

In-depth examination of specific cancer cases demonstrates the application of genomic insights in clinical settings, showcasing personalized treatment strategies and outcomes based on genomic profiling.
